{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    COMBINED PYTHON GUIDE
    Python Best Practices & Python Testing Frameworks
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: Python Best Practices
  1.1 Code Style & PEP 8
  1.2 Project Structure & Organization
  1.3 Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
  1.4 Type Hints & Documentation
  1.5 Error Handling & Logging
  1.6 Performance Considerations
  1.7 Security Best Practices

PART 2: Python Testing Frameworks
  2.1 Why Testing Matters
  2.2 unittest (Built-in)
  2.3 pytest
  2.4 doctest
  2.5 nose2
  2.6 Framework Comparison Table
  2.7 Testing Tools Ecosystem (Mock, Coverage, Tox)
  2.8 Testing Best Practices

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & PEP 8
-----------------------
PEP 8 is the official style guide for Python code. Key guidelines include:

  - Indentation: Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
  - Line Length: Maximum 79 characters per line (72 for docstrings/comments).
  - Blank Lines: Use two blank lines around top-level functions/classes;
    one blank line around method definitions inside a class.
  - Imports: Each import on its own line; group in order:
      1. Standard library imports
      2. Third-party imports
      3. Local application imports
  - Naming Conventions:
      - Classes: CapWords (CamelCase) e.g., MyClass
      - Functions/Variables: snake_case e.g., my_function
      - Constants: UPPER_CASE e.g., MAX_VALUE
      - Private attributes: _leading_underscore
      - Name mangling: __double_leading_underscore
  - Whitespace: Avoid extraneous whitespace inside parentheses, brackets,
    braces, or before commas.

Tools to enforce PEP 8:
  - flake8: Linter that checks PEP 8 compliance
  - black: Opinionated code formatter (auto-formats to PEP 8)
  - isort: Sorts imports automatically
  - pylint: Comprehensive linter with style checks

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE & ORGANIZATION
--------------------------------------
Recommended project layout for a typical Python project:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern project config (PEP 621)
    ├── setup.py                # (Optional) Legacy setup script
    ├── setup.cfg               # (Optional) Legacy config
    ├── requirements.txt        # Production dependencies
    ├── requirements-dev.txt    # Development dependencies
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module1.py
    │       ├── module2.py
    │       └── subpackage/
    │           ├── __init__.py
    │           └── ...
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module1.py
    │   ├── test_module2.py
    │   └── conftest.py         # pytest configuration
    ├── docs/
    │   └── ...
    └── scripts/
        └── ...

Key principles:
  - One class/module per file (when sensible).
  - Keep modules small and focused (Single Responsibility Principle).
  - Use packages (directories with __init__.py) to organize related modules.
  - Separate source code from tests.

1.3 VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS & DEPENDENCY MANAGEMENT
--------------------------------------------------
Always use virtual environments to isolate project dependencies.

  - venv (built-in):
      python -m venv venv
      source venv/bin/activate   # Linux/Mac
      venv\Scripts\activate      # Windows

  - pipenv: Combines pip and virtualenv with a Pipfile.
  - poetry: Modern dependency management with pyproject.toml.
  - conda: For data science projects with non-Python dependencies.

Dependency files:
  - requirements.txt: Pin exact versions for reproducible builds.
      flask==3.0.0
      requests==2.31.0
  - requirements-dev.txt: Include testing
